content_of_post_to_comment: When people say, “Everything is a choice.” I always wonder if they’ve forgotten that some people were born into war. Some grew up in violent homes. Some spent childhood walking on eggshells around addiction, abuse, neglect, or chaos. Some were taught guilt before boundaries. Fear before safety. Survival before self-worth. No, we don’t choose where we begin. We don’t choose the family we’re born into, the violence we witness, the trauma we inherit, or the beliefs we absorb before we’re old enough to question them. The choice comes later. The choice is whether we’re willing to examine it, heal it, and stop passing it on. So before throwing “everything is a choice” around like confetti, remember that not everyone started from the same place. Some people had to survive things you may never have experienced. That’s a very different conversation.
comment: The phrase “the choice comes later” raises an interesting question: healing itself often depends on resources, relationships, and luck. At what point does responsibility begin when access to recovery isn’t evenly distributed either?
